2	title: Caching and Revalidating
3	description: Learn how to cache and revalidate data in your application.
4	related:
5	  title: API Reference
6	  description: Learn more about the features mentioned in this page by reading the API Reference.
7	  links:
8	    - app/api-reference/functions/fetch
9	    - app/api-reference/functions/cacheTag
10	    - app/api-reference/functions/revalidateTag
11	    - app/api-reference/functions/updateTag
12	    - app/api-reference/functions/revalidatePath
13	    - app/api-reference/functions/unstable_cache
14	---
15	
16	Caching is a technique for storing the result of data fetching and other computations so that future requests for the same data can be served faster, without doing the work again. While revalidation allows you to update cache entries without having to rebuild your entire application.
17	
18	Next.js provides a few APIs to handle caching and revalidation. This guide will walk you through when and how to use them.
19	
20	- [`fetch`](#fetch)
21	- [`cacheTag`](#cachetag)
22	- [`revalidateTag`](#revalidatetag)
23	- [`updateTag`](#updatetag)
24	- [`revalidatePath`](#revalidatepath)
25	- [`unstable_cache`](#unstable_cache) (Legacy)
26	
27	## `fetch`
28	
29	By default, [`fetch`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/fetch) requests are not cached. You can cache individual requests by setting the `cache` option to `'force-cache'`.
30	
31	```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" switcher
32	export default async function Page() {
33	  const data = await fetch('https://...', { cache: 'force-cache' })
34	}
35	```
36	
37	```jsx filename="app/page.jsx" switcher
38	export default async function Page() {
39	  const data = await fetch('https://...', { cache: 'force-cache' })
40	}
41	```
42	
43	> **Good to know**: Although `fetch` requests are not cached by default, Next.js will [pre-render](/docs/app/guides/caching#static-rendering) routes that have `fetch` requests and cache the HTML. If you want to guarantee a route is [dynamic](/docs/app/guides/caching#dynamic-rendering), use the [`connection` API](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/connection).
44	
45	To revalidate the data returned by a `fetch` request, you can use the `next.revalidate` option.
46	
47	```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" switcher
48	export default async function Page() {
49	  const data = await fetch('https://...', { next: { revalidate: 3600 } })
50	}
51	```
52	
53	```jsx filename="app/page.jsx" switcher
54	export default async function Page() {
55	  const data = await fetch('https://...', { next: { revalidate: 3600 } })
56	}
57	```
58	
59	This will revalidate the data after a specified amount of seconds.
60	
61	You can also tag `fetch` requests to enable on-demand cache invalidation:
62	
63	```tsx filename="app/lib/data.ts" switcher
64	export async function getUserById(id: string) {
65	  const data = await fetch(`https://...`, {
66	    next: {
67	      tags: ['user'],
68	    },
69	  })
70	}
71	```
72	
73	```jsx filename="app/lib/data.js" switcher
74	export async function getUserById(id) {
75	  const data = await fetch(`https://...`, {
76	    next: {
77	      tags: ['user'],
78	    },
79	  })
80	}
81	```
82	
83	See the [`fetch` API reference](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/fetch) to learn more.
84	
85	## `cacheTag`
86	
87	[`cacheTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cacheTag) allows you to tag cached data in [Cache Components](/docs/app/getting-started/cache-components) so it can be revalidated on-demand. Previously, cache tagging was limited to `fetch` requests, and caching other work required the experimental `unstable_cache` API.
88	
89	With Cache Components, you can use the [`use cache`](/docs/app/api-reference/directives/use-cache) directive to cache any computation, and `cacheTag` to tag it. This works with database queries, file system operations, and other server-side work.
90	
91	```tsx filename="app/lib/data.ts" switcher
92	import { cacheTag } from 'next/cache'
93

2	title: Updating Data
3	description: Learn how to mutate data using Server Functions and Server Actions in Next.js.
4	related:
5	  title: API Reference
6	  description: Learn more about the features mentioned in this page by reading the API Reference.
7	  links:
8	    - app/api-reference/functions/revalidatePath
9	    - app/api-reference/functions/revalidateTag
10	    - app/api-reference/functions/redirect
11	---
12	
13	You can update data in Next.js using React's [Server Functions](https://react.dev/reference/rsc/server-functions). This page will go through how you can [create](#creating-server-functions) and [invoke](#invoking-server-functions) Server Functions.
14	
15	## What are Server Functions?
16	
17	A **Server Function** is an asynchronous function that runs on the server. They can be called from the client through a network request, which is why they must be asynchronous.
18	
19	In an `action` or mutation context, they are also called **Server Actions**.
20	
21	> **Good to know:** A Server Action is a Server Function used in a specific way (for handling form submissions and mutations). Server Function is the broader term.
22	
23	By convention, a Server Action is an async function used with [`startTransition`](https://react.dev/reference/react/startTransition). This happens automatically when the function is:
24	
25	- Passed to a `<form>` using the `action` prop.
26	- Passed to a `<button>` using the `formAction` prop.
27	
28	In Next.js, Server Actions integrate with the framework's [caching](/docs/app/guides/caching) architecture. When an action is invoked, Next.js can return both the updated UI and new data in a single server roundtrip.
29	
30	Behind the scenes, actions use the `POST` method, and only this HTTP method can invoke them.
31	
32	## Creating Server Functions
33	
34	A Server Function can be defined by using the [`use server`](https://react.dev/reference/rsc/use-server) directive. You can place the directive at the top of an **asynchronous** function to mark the function as a Server Function, or at the top of a separate file to mark all exports of that file.
35	
36	```ts filename="app/lib/actions.ts" switcher
37	export async function createPost(formData: FormData) {
38	  'use server'
39	  const title = formData.get('title')
40	  const content = formData.get('content')
41	
42	  // Update data
43	  // Revalidate cache
44	}
45	
46	export async function deletePost(formData: FormData) {
47	  'use server'
48	  const id = formData.get('id')
49	
50	  // Update data
51	  // Revalidate cache
52	}
53	```
54	
55	```js filename="app/lib/actions.js" switcher
56	export async function createPost(formData) {
57	  'use server'
58	  const title = formData.get('title')
59	  const content = formData.get('content')
60	
61	  // Update data
62	  // Revalidate cache
63	}
64	
65	export async function deletePost(formData) {
66	  'use server'
67	  const id = formData.get('id')
68	
69	  // Update data
70	  // Revalidate cache
71	}
72	```
73	
74	### Server Components
75	
76	Server Functions can be inlined in Server Components by adding the `"use server"` directive to the top of the function body:
77	
78	```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" switcher
79	export default function Page() {
80	  // Server Action
81	  async function createPost(formData: FormData) {
82	    'use server'
83	    // ...
84	  }
85	
86	  return <></>
87	}
88	```
89	
90	```jsx filename="app/page.js" switcher
91	export default function Page() {
92	  // Server Action
93	  async function createPost(formData: FormData) {
94	    'use server'
95	    // ...
96	  }
97	
98	  return <></>
99	}
100	```
101	
102	> **Good to know:** Server Components support progressive enhancement by default, meaning forms that call Server Actions will be submitted even if JavaScript hasn't loaded yet or is disabled.
103	
104	### Client Components

2	title: updateTag
3	description: API Reference for the updateTag function.
4	---
5	
6	`updateTag` allows you to update [cached data](/docs/app/guides/caching) on-demand for a specific cache tag from within [Server Actions](/docs/app/getting-started/updating-data).
7	
8	This function is designed for **read-your-own-writes** scenarios, where a user makes a change (like creating a post), and the UI immediately shows the change, rather than stale data.
9	
10	## Usage
11	
12	`updateTag` can **only** be called from within [Server Actions](/docs/app/getting-started/updating-data). It cannot be used in Route Handlers, Client Components, or any other context.
13	
14	If you need to invalidate cache tags in Route Handlers or other contexts, use [`revalidateTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidateTag) instead.
15	
16	> **Good to know**: `updateTag` immediately expires the cached data for the specified tag. The next request will wait to fetch fresh data rather than serving stale content from the cache, ensuring users see their changes immediately.
17	
18	## Parameters
19	
20	```tsx
21	updateTag(tag: string): void;
22	```
23	
24	- `tag`: A string representing the cache tag associated with the data you want to update. Must not exceed 256 characters. This value is case-sensitive.
25	
26	Tags must first be assigned to cached data. You can do this in two ways:
27	
28	- Using the [`next.tags`](/docs/app/guides/caching#fetch-optionsnexttags-and-revalidatetag) option with `fetch` for caching external API requests:
29	
30	```tsx
31	fetch(url, { next: { tags: ['posts'] } })
32	```
33	
34	- Using [`cacheTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cacheTag) inside cached functions or components with the `'use cache'` directive:
35	
36	```tsx
37	import { cacheTag } from 'next/cache'
38	
39	async function getData() {
40	  'use cache'
41	  cacheTag('posts')
42	  // ...
43	}
44	```
45	
46	## Returns
47	
48	`updateTag` does not return a value.
49	
50	## Differences from revalidateTag
51	
52	While both `updateTag` and `revalidateTag` invalidate cached data, they serve different purposes:
53	
54	- **`updateTag`**:
55	  - Can only be used in Server Actions
56	  - Next request waits for fresh data (no stale content served)
57	  - Designed for read-your-own-writes scenarios
58	
59	- **`revalidateTag`**:
60	  - Can be used in Server Actions and Route Handlers
61	  - With `profile="max"` (recommended): Serves cached data while fetching fresh data in the background (stale-while-revalidate)
62	  - With custom profile: Can be configured to any cache life profile for advanced usage
63	  - Without profile: legacy behavior which is equivalent to `updateTag`
64	
65	## Examples
66	
67	### Server Action with Read-Your-Own-Writes
68	
69	```ts filename="app/actions.ts" switcher
70	'use server'
71	
72	import { updateTag } from 'next/cache'
73	import { redirect } from 'next/navigation'
74	
75	export async function createPost(formData: FormData) {
76	  const title = formData.get('title')
77	  const content = formData.get('content')
78	
79	  // Create the post in your database
80	  const post = await db.post.create({
81	    data: { title, content },
82	  })
83	
84	  // Invalidate cache tags so the new post is immediately visible
85	  // 'posts' tag: affects any page that displays a list of posts
86	  updateTag('posts')
87	  // 'post-{id}' tag: affects the individual post detail page
88	  updateTag(`post-${post.id}`)
89	
90	  // Redirect to the new post - user will see fresh data, not cached
91	  redirect(`/posts/${post.id}`)
92	}
93	```
94

97	
98	import { updateTag } from 'next/cache'
99	import { redirect } from 'next/navigation'
100

102	  const title = formData.get('title')
103	  const content = formData.get('content')
104	
105	  // Create the post in your database
106	  const post = await db.post.create({
107	    data: { title, content },
108	  })
109

13	
14	The `cacheTag` function allows you to tag cached data for on-demand invalidation. By associating tags with cache entries, you can selectively purge or revalidate specific cache entries without affecting other cached data.
15	
16	## Usage
17	
18	To use `cacheTag`, enable the [`cacheComponents` flag](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/cacheComponents) in your `next.config.js` file:
19	
20	```ts filename="next.config.ts" switcher
21	import type { NextConfig } from 'next'
22	
23	const nextConfig: NextConfig = {
24	  cacheComponents: true,
25	}
26	
27	export default nextConfig
28	```
29	
30	```js filename="next.config.js" switcher
31	const nextConfig = {
32	  cacheComponents: true,
33	}
34	
35	export default nextConfig
36	```
37	
38	The `cacheTag` function takes one or more string values.
39	
40	```tsx filename="app/data.ts" switcher
41	import { cacheTag } from 'next/cache'
42	
43	export async function getData() {
44	  'use cache'
45	  cacheTag('my-data')
46	  const data = await fetch('/api/data')
47	  return data
48	}
49	```
50	
51	```jsx filename="app/data.js" switcher
52	import { cacheTag } from 'next/cache'
53	
54	export async function getData() {
55	  'use cache'
56	  cacheTag('my-data')
57	  const data = await fetch('/api/data')
58	  return data
59	}
60	```
61

87	
88	- **Idempotent Tags**: Applying the same tag multiple times has no additional effect.

91	```tsx

2	title: revalidateTag
3	description: API Reference for the revalidateTag function.
4	---
5	
6	`revalidateTag` allows you to invalidate [cached data](/docs/app/guides/caching) on-demand for a specific cache tag.
7	
8	This function is ideal for content where a slight delay in updates is acceptable, such as blog posts, product catalogs, or documentation. Users receive stale content while fresh data loads in the background.
9	
10	## Usage
11	
12	`revalidateTag` can be called in Server Functions and Route Handlers.
13	
14	`revalidateTag` cannot be called in Client Components or Proxy, as it only works in server environments.
15	
16	### Revalidation Behavior
17	
18	The revalidation behavior depends on whether you provide the second argument:
19	
20	- **With `profile="max"` (recommended)**: The tag entry is marked as stale, and the next time a resource with that tag is visited, it will use stale-while-revalidate semantics. This means the stale content is served while fresh content is fetched in the background.
21	- **With a custom cache life profile**: For advanced usage, you can specify any cache life profile that your application has defined, allowing for custom revalidation behaviors tailored to your specific caching requirements.
22	- **Without the second argument (deprecated)**: The tag entry is expired immediately, and the next request to that resource will be a blocking revalidate/cache miss. This behavior is now deprecated, and you should either use `profile="max"` or migrate to [`updateTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/updateTag).
23	
24	> **Good to know**: When using `profile="max"`, `revalidateTag` marks tagged data as stale, but fresh data is only fetched when pages using that tag are next visited. This means calling `revalidateTag` will not immediately trigger many revalidations at once. The invalidation only happens when any page using that tag is next visited.
25	
26	## Parameters
27	
28	```ts
29	revalidateTag(tag: string, profile: string | { expire?: number }): void;
30	```
31	
32	- `tag`: A string representing the cache tag associated with the data you want to revalidate. Must not exceed 256 characters. This value is case-sensitive.
33	- `profile`: A string that specifies the revalidation behavior. The recommended value is `"max"` which provides stale-while-revalidate semantics, or any of the other default or custom profiles defined in [`cacheLife`](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/cacheLife). Alternatively, you can pass an object with an `expire` property for custom expiration behavior.
34	
35	Tags must first be assigned to cached data. You can do this in two ways:
36	
37	- Using the [`next.tags`](/docs/app/guides/caching#fetch-optionsnexttags-and-revalidatetag) option with `fetch` for caching external API requests:
38	
39	```tsx
40	fetch(url, { next: { tags: ['posts'] } })
41	```
42	
43	- Using [`cacheTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cacheTag) inside cached functions or components with the `'use cache'` directive:
44	
45	```tsx
46	import { cacheTag } from 'next/cache'
47	
48	async function getData() {
49	  'use cache'
50	  cacheTag('posts')
51	  // ...
52	}
53	```
54	
55	> **Good to know**: The single-argument form `revalidateTag(tag)` is deprecated. It currently works if TypeScript errors are suppressed, but this behavior may be removed in a future version. Update to the two-argument signature.
56	
57	## Returns
58	
59	`revalidateTag` does not return a value.
60	
61	## Relationship with `revalidatePath`
62	
63	`revalidateTag` invalidates data with specific tags across all pages that use those tags, while [`revalidatePath`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidatePath) invalidates specific page or layout paths.
64	
65	> **Good to know**: These functions serve different purposes and may need to be used together for comprehensive data consistency. For detailed examples and considerations, see [relationship with revalidateTag and updateTag](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalid…(+2383)

15	
16	### Revalidation Behavior
17	
18	The revalidation behavior depends on whether you provide the second argument:
19	
20	- **With `profile="max"` (recommended)**: The tag entry is marked as stale, and the next time a resource with that tag is visited, it will use stale-while-revalidate semantics. This means the stale content is served while fresh content is fetched in the background.

This behavior is now deprecated, and you should either use `profile="max"` or migrate to [`updateTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/updateTag).
23	
24	> **Good to know**: When using `profile="max"`, `revalidateTag` marks tagged data as stale, but fresh data is only fetched when pages using that tag are next visited. This means calling `revalidateTag` will not immediately trigger many revalidations at once. The invalidation only happens when any page using that tag is next visited.
25	
26	## Parameters
27	
28	```ts
29	revalidateTag(tag: string, profile: string | { expire?: number }): void;
30	```
31	
32	- `tag`: A string representing the cache tag associated with the data you want to revalidate. Must not exceed 256 characters. This value is case-sensitive.
33	- `profile`: A string that specifies the revalidation behavior. The recommended value is `"max"` which provides stale-while-revalidate semantics, or any of the other default or custom profiles defined in [`cacheLife`](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/cacheLife). Alternatively, you can pass an object with an `expire` property for custom expiration behavior.

35	Tags must first be assigned to cached data. You can do this in two ways:
36	
37	- Using the [`next.tags`](/docs/app/guides/caching#fetch-optionsnexttags-and-revalidatetag) option with `fetch` for caching external API requests:

41	```

50	  cacheTag('posts')
51	  // ...
52	}
53	```

56	
57	## Returns
58	
59	`revalidateTag` does not return a value.
60	
61	## Relationship with `revalidatePath`
62	
63	`revalidateTag` invalidates data with specific tags across all pages that use those tags, while [`revalidatePath`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidatePath) invalidates specific page or layout paths.

1	---
2	title: cacheComponents
3	description: Learn how to enable the cacheComponents flag in Next.js.
4	---
5	
6	The `cacheComponents` flag is a feature in Next.js that causes data fetching operations in the App Router to be excluded from pre-renders unless they are explicitly cached. This can be useful for optimizing the performance of dynamic data fetching in Server Components.
7	
8	It is useful if your application requires fresh data fetching during runtime rather than serving from a pre-rendered cache.
9	
10	It is expected to be used in conjunction with [`use cache`](/docs/app/api-reference/directives/use-cache) so that your data fetching happens at runtime by default unless you define specific parts of your application to be cached with `use cache` at the page, function, or component level.
11	
12	## Usage
13	
14	To enable the `cacheComponents` flag, set it to `true` in your `next.config.ts` file:
15	
16	```ts filename="next.config.ts"
17	import type { NextConfig } from 'next'
18	
19	const nextConfig: NextConfig = {
20	  cacheComponents: true,
21	}
22	
23	export default nextConfig
24	```
25	
26	When `cacheComponents` is enabled, you can use the following cache functions and configurations:
27	
28	- The [`use cache` directive](/docs/app/api-reference/directives/use-cache)
29	- The [`cacheLife` function](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/cacheLife) with `use cache`
30	- The [`cacheTag` function](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cacheTag)
31	
32	## Notes
33	
34	- While `cacheComponents` can optimize performance by ensuring fresh data fetching during runtime, it may also introduce additional latency compared to serving pre-rendered content.
35	
36	## Version History
37	
38	| Version | Change                                                                                                                            |
39	| ------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
40	| 16.0.0  | `cacheComponents` introduced. This flag controls the `ppr`, `useCache`, and `dynamicIO` flags as a single, unified configuration. |
41

2	title: use cache
3	description: Learn how to use the "use cache" directive to cache data in your Next.js application.
4	related:
5	  title: Related
6	  description: View related API references.
7	  links:
8	    - app/api-reference/directives/use-cache-private
9	    - app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/cacheComponents
10	    - app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/cacheLife
11	    - app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/cacheHandlers
12	    - app/api-reference/functions/cacheTag
13	    - app/api-reference/functions/cacheLife
14	    - app/api-reference/functions/revalidateTag
15	---
16	
17	The `use cache` directive allows you to mark a route, React component, or a function as cacheable. It can be used at the top of a file to indicate that all exports in the file should be cached, or inline at the top of function or component to cache the return value.
18	
19	> **Good to know:**
20	>
21	> - To use cookies or headers, read them outside cached scopes and pass values as arguments. This is the preferred pattern.
22	> - If the in-memory cache isn't sufficient for runtime data, [`'use cache: remote'`](/docs/app/api-reference/directives/use-cache-remote) allows platforms to provide a dedicated cache handler, though it requires a network roundtrip to check the cache and typically incurs platform fees.
23	> - For compliance requirements or when you can't refactor to pass runtime data as arguments to a `use cache` scope, see [`'use cache: private'`](/docs/app/api-reference/directives/use-cache-private).
24	
25	## Usage
26	
27	`use cache` is a Cache Components feature. To enable it, add the [`cacheComponents`](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/cacheComponents) option to your `next.config.ts` file:
28	
29	```ts filename="next.config.ts" switcher
30	import type { NextConfig } from 'next'
31	
32	const nextConfig: NextConfig = {
33	  cacheComponents: true,
34	}
35	
36	export default nextConfig
37	```
38	
39	```js filename="next.config.js" switcher
40	/** @type {import('next').NextConfig} */
41	const nextConfig = {
42	  cacheComponents: true,
43	}
44	
45	module.exports = nextConfig
46	```
47	
48	Then, add `use cache` at the file, component, or function level:
49	
50	```tsx
51	// File level
52	'use cache'
53	
54	export default async function Page() {
55	  // ...
56	}
57	
58	// Component level
59	export async function MyComponent() {
60	  'use cache'
61	  return <></>
62	}
63	
64	// Function level
65	export async function getData() {
66	  'use cache'
67	  const data = await fetch('/api/data')
68	  return data
69	}
70	```
71	
72	> **Good to know**: When used at file level, all function exports must be async functions.
73	
74	## How `use cache` works
75	
76	### Cache keys
77	
78	A cache entry's key is generated using a serialized version of its inputs, which includes:
79	
80	1. **Build ID** - Unique per build, changing this invalidates all cache entries
81	2. **Function ID** - A secure hash of the function's location and signature in the codebase
82	3. **Serializable arguments** - Props (for components) or function arguments
83	4. **HMR refresh hash** (development only) - Invalidates cache on hot module replacement
84	
85	When a cached function references variables from outer scopes, those variables are automatically captured and bound as arguments, making them part of the cache key.
86	
87	```tsx filename="lib/data.ts"
88	async function Component({ userId }: { userId: string }) {
89	  const getData = async (filter: string) => {
90	    'use cache'
91	    // Cache key includes both userId (from closure) and filter (argument)
92	    return fetch(`/api/users/${userId}/data?filter=${filter}`)
93	  }
94	
95	  return getData('active')
96	}
97	```
98	
99	In the snippet above, `userId` is captured from the outer scope and `filter` is passed as an argument, so both become part of the `getData` function's cache key. This means different user and filter combinations will have separate cache entries.
100	
101	## Serialization
102
